# Chapter 1

[lbh1mt40] Cage hit the guardrail at a hundred and thirty and the car did not so much stop as change its mind about being a car. The nose went under. The back came up. Metal tore like cloth. The world turned end over end, desert and sky, the pale smear of a solar farm, then nothing but sound.

[1vk11tgw] He tasted blood and copper and the dust that lived in the seams of the dashboard. The harness had cut him in two places. His ribs were not ribs anymore. He could feel air in his chest where air had no business being, bubbling, wet.

[hvjp1ei8] The Shelby came to rest on its side in a ditch full of crushed beer cans and tumbleweed. The engine ticked and made a thin, patient hiss. Heat rolled off the hood. Somewhere a bird kept calling like it had not noticed the wreck.

[my941lls] Cage blinked. The windshield was gone. The desert wind moved through the cabin like a hand. He could not move his legs. He could not feel his feet at all.

[be6qozmg] His implant chimed in his skull. A calm voice, not his, said: *Critical event detected.* The words did not come through his ears. They wrote themselves in the soft place behind his eyes.

[rva6cegp] A translucent banner slid across his vision. **SPONSORED ALERT:** *Stay hydrated this season with AguaPure™ electrolyte gels.* A cheerful droplet icon pulsed beside the text. It blocked the view of the ditch.

[vng9iuhq] He laughed once. It came out as a cough. Something warm ran down his throat. He tried to spit and could not.

[hqngz3au] *Location acquired,* the voice said. *Dispatching emergency unit. Do not attempt to stand. Breathe slowly.*

[27y36t8k] He could not breathe slowly. Every breath was a knife. He stared at the washed-out sky and waited for the pain to finish him like it always promised.

[ru494hcm] The pain did not finish him. It only counted time.

[911937hv] His hands were still on the steering wheel. The wheel was bent. The emblem in the center looked like a coin someone had tried to flatten with a hammer. He thought of his father for no reason. A man in a white tank top. A cigarette. A laugh.

[pd9i0k0c] The implant chimed again. Another banner. **LIMITED TIME:** *Upgrade to Premium VitalWatch™ for uninterrupted emergency guidance.* The ad had a small X in the corner that floated just out of reach of his gaze.

[pyy3spch] He tried to roll his eyes and only managed to make his vision swim. He wondered if this was how people died now. With coupons.

[pewpzj8y] He heard the whir before he saw anything. The medical drone came in low over the highway, a fat white insect with four rotors and a red cross that had been made into a logo. It hovered for a moment as if deciding whether he was worth the battery.

[y4hnw0b1] A cable dropped. A pod came down the cable, coffin-shaped, matte gray, with a clear lid that reflected the sky. It touched the dirt with a soft thud. The side split open.

[voc66eup] A mechanized arm reached into the wreck and cut his harness in two strokes. Another arm sprayed foam that turned the blood bright black. The arms moved with a tenderness no human had.

[eogzxwkk] *Cardiac arrest imminent,* the voice said.

[4voa8gzi] Cage tried to speak. He wanted to say *good.* He wanted to say *let it.* The only sound was a small animal sound.

[cfjytae1] The arms lifted him. His body dragged against the torn doorframe and something in him snagged and let go. The sky tilted. The desert blurred.

[0h2uuhcl] Inside the pod it smelled like antiseptic and warm plastic. Cold gel met his skin. The lid closed above him. It was inches from his face.

[v6zljkg2] The display on the inside of the lid lit up with a friendly blue interface. A cartoon heart bounced gently in the corner. **WELCOME BACK, CAGE.** He had never told it his name. It knew anyway.

[t3p0tovh] The pod clamped down on his chest and the world narrowed to a bright point. He felt his heart stumble, then stop, like a car stalling at a light.

[khpft4gf] There was a clean moment with no sound at all. The pain disappeared. The desert disappeared. He thought, without words: *finally.*

[vgnaiz2y] Then the point widened into a hallway of light and noise and the pod hit him with electricity and drugs and air. The heart jumped like a hooked fish. His eyes opened so hard they hurt.

[ju4iei4d] The interface bloomed with confetti. **SUCCESS!** **DEATH EVENT RESOLVED.** Under it, a smaller line: *Please rate your resuscitation experience.*

[65ue0vjm] He screamed, or meant to. The pod filled his lungs for him. The gel in his throat turned his scream into a wet rattle.

[np9ivpqm] A countdown appeared in the corner. *Mandatory post-event stabilization: 03:59… 03:58…*

[l7tmkyn4] He fought the straps. The pod tightened like a fist. A message appeared: *Unauthorized movement detected. For your safety—*

[1mjvmisp] **SPONSORED MESSAGE:** *Try CalmNow™ breathing exercises. Four seconds in. Four seconds out.* A serene woman smiled in the ad, eyes closed, palms up.

[ptacyci9] Cage stared at the woman until the ad timed out. When it cleared, the pod showed him his own vitals, bright and perfect, like a lie written in numbers.

[fpaozb6f] He lay still and listened to the soft mechanical noises. Somewhere in the pod something stitched. Somewhere it pumped blood through new tubing. He could feel metal under his skin where bone had been. He could feel the places he would ache when the drugs wore off.

[jdamzpfs] He counted the minutes down not because he needed to, but because it was the only thing in the universe that agreed to move forward.

[8z4az48s] When the timer hit zero the lid popped up and warm desert air washed in. A sunshade extended. The pod's arm offered him a bottle of water branded with AguaPure™.

[e9dtpwkp] He sat up too fast. The world pitched. He tasted bile. His hands shook as if they belonged to someone else.

[tjededzl] On the highway above him a convoy of electric pods slid past in a neat line. No drivers. No noise but tire hush. They did not slow for the wreck. The drones had already flagged it as resolved.

[yxjcg9ws] A patrol vehicle arrived late, a white city pod with blue lights that were as quiet as the rest of it. A man in a thin uniform stepped out. He did not look at Cage so much as at the tablet in his hand.

[ppnmbpzk] "Name?" the man said.

[3pimkn02] "Cage." His voice came out rough.

[seizamja] The man nodded, as if that was not a name but a file. "You have outstanding revival fees from the last event. Standard policy is transport to a clinic for payment arrangement."

[p0pro4em] Cage stood. His knees wobbled but held. He pulled the IV lines out of his arm with two quick jerks. Blood dotted the dirt.

[vf2ocl5f] The man flinched like he was watching an animal do something unpredictable. "Sir. You need to remain—"

[5m6u7fsg] "No." Cage looked up at the desert. The heat shimmered over the asphalt. Far away the boneyard cranes stood like dead birds. "You can keep your pod."

[p8x2idks] "It's mandated." The man lifted his tablet. "It's for your safety."

[9nnvqhia] Cage smiled at him. It was not kind. "Safety is a leash." He started walking.

[44gqsavq] Behind him the drone hovered, waiting for an instruction it would not get. The officer called after him once, twice. Cage did not turn. The implant tried to display another ad and he shut his eyes until it went away.

[i8l5mjlb] He walked along the shoulder of the highway toward the broken car in the ditch. Smoke rose in a thin line. The smell of gasoline and burned rubber was the only honest thing in the air.

[wazje3yu] He reached the Shelby and put his hand on the torn fender. The metal was still warm. He closed his eyes. He listened to the ticking as it cooled.

[q2pkjq0i] "Four minutes," he said to no one. "Four minutes dead and they drag you back like a dog."

[jkf6twgr] He opened his eyes and looked at the empty road beyond the shimmer. Somewhere out there the desert kept its own ledger. It did not offer discounts. It did not apologize. It only waited.
[49iibjts] The officer let him go because the tablet told him to. The drone had done its work. The case was closed. A human man with a thin voice was not going to wrestle a revived outlaw on the side of a highway.

[ntwmo14o] Cage climbed into the wreck and pulled the glove box open. Inside were old registration papers and a folded map that had been printed on paper so thin it looked like skin. The map was older than the ban. It smelled like oil.

[8mo9ee4p] He took a flare from the center console. He struck it and the red light made the ditch look like the inside of a wound. He held it to the seat. The upholstery caught and ran.

[076uanct] The car had been his once. That mattered. It mattered the way bones mattered. Now it was a beacon for drones and a bill he didn't intend to pay.

[oy3ri5ox] His implant tried to warn him about air quality. It drew a neat little graph over the flames. He blinked it away.

[i891dvvi] He walked until the highway noise fell behind him and the desert sound came back. Wind in the creosote. A far truck. The faint crackle of his burning car like someone chewing.

[vjqo9sby] The pod that had revived him followed on its cable for a while, hovering overhead, unwilling to abandon its patient. Finally it cut power to the rotors and sank back toward the wreck, as if ashamed.

[51lmbeg5] His chest hurt in a way the drugs could not smooth. Every step pulled at the stitches the pod had laid down inside him. He felt the new parts like strangers.

[20mof7ju] He had died before. Twice that counted. Once in a street race outside Tucson when a kid in a hacked pod drifted wide and hit him. Once in a garage when a fuel cook went wrong and the air turned into fire. The first time he woke up crying. The second time he woke up angry.

[aznsqx5x] He did not remember the moment of dying. He remembered the moments after. The room too bright. The voices too cheerful. The way everyone spoke to him like he was lucky.

[ts84sq1b] Lucky. Like a man dragged out of a river was lucky to be wet and shivering and alive.

[qgtwt1sf] A mile from the crash site there was a low building half buried in sand. A clinic outpost. A white sign with green letters: **DESERT REGIONAL REVIVAL - WALK-INS WELCOME.** The word *welcome* had been replaced twice.

[f4nlzpwj] Inside it was cool. The air smelled like disinfectant and synthetic citrus. A woman behind a counter looked up. Her eyes flicked to his face and then to the blood on his shirt and then back to a screen.

[2c1p251o] "Do you have your revival receipt?" she asked.

[h4d6fih7] "I have a pulse," Cage said.

[anu57k1s] She did not smile. "We need to close the event and reconcile your implant logs. Sit."

[ckamc76s] The waiting room held six chairs bolted to the floor. Two people sat there already. A man with his forearm in a printed brace, staring at the wall. A girl with shaved hair and a broken nose, scrolling with her eyes through whatever ads her implant couldn't block.

[u0qe50np] A screen on the wall played public service clips in a loop. How to recognize a heat stroke. How to report illegal combustion. How to breathe during panic. Each clip had a sponsor.

[nte9rihk] Cage sat because the chairs were bolted and so was his body. His ribs felt like someone had replaced them with thin glass.

[8txh5pt3] The shaved-haired girl glanced at him. She had the flat calm of someone who had woken up too many times. "You racing?" she asked.

[obqrgfz1] "No," he said.

[0gqn3sy0] She nodded like that answered nothing and everything. "Me neither." She went back to her ads.

[agwpn3r1] A door opened and a medic called the man with the brace. The medic's implant projected a soft blue halo around his head, a corporate aura. Cage watched him disappear down the hallway.

[ey16kur8] His own implant buzzed. **OFFER:** *Finance your revival fees with EasyPay™. Low interest. No judgment.* The words sat over the clinic floor tiles.

[axizs2uw] He stood up and walked to the counter. The woman looked annoyed that he was moving without permission.

[niur1noc] "I need a ride," he said. "My car's gone."

[7kvy1gd0] "Transportation is provided," she said. "To a stabilization ward. Then to your registered domicile."

[td8zty4y] "I don't have one."

[wwli6i1l] Her fingers paused over the screen. For the first time she looked at him like he was a problem instead of a line item. "Everyone has an address."

[bo92w3e7] Cage leaned closer. "Not everyone."

[ev7hhb9x] She clicked her tongue and typed. "Unregistered. Fine. You'll be routed to overflow services. Do not leave."

[52i7ie2n] "Or what?" he asked.

[5p2ts1nh] She did not answer. She gestured to a small camera in the corner like a priest pointing at God.

[vs1oxugo] He went back to the chair. His hands shook. Not fear. Not cold. Just his nerves trying to remember their jobs.

[1mo62jnf] In the hallway he heard someone crying. The sound was cut off mid sob by a chirp and then an ad voice. The crying resumed after, smaller.

[v4z34s57] He thought of his father again. Not the father he had, but the father he imagined other men had. A man who would have let him die. A man who would have said: *you chose it.*

[8mk01nst] The door opened and the medic called his name. "Cage." Like it was on a leash too.

[8k74cd9n] They took him to a small room with a chair and a monitor. The monitor showed his internal repairs in simple icons. It showed the stitched artery like a blue line. It showed the pins in his pelvis like nails.

[c0irt259] "Any lingering confusion? Suicidal ideation?" the medic asked, voice bored.

[88seg9qg] "If I say yes you'll lock me up," Cage said.

[drrm1a3u] The medic shrugged. "If you say yes the system locks you up. I just click."

[zs9o0ii2] "Then no."

[tws1b8ab] "Pain level?"

[oqvxmukg] "High."

[e5dhlpiv] The medic nodded and offered him a small packet. **Analgesic gummies**. The packet had a cartoon cactus smiling.

[o9k7z6ro] "Sign here," the medic said.

[3b16ngc8] "I don't sign," Cage said.

[o7dzo4rt] "Then you don't get the meds and your implant will flag noncompliance."

[2aw085gf] Cage took the packet and tore it open with his teeth. The gummies were chalky and sweet. He chewed them like they were a punishment.

[z055yuez] The medic started reading him a script about legal obligations. About prohibited risk behaviors. About how the state cared.

[em0se7ze] Cage listened until the script reached the part about mandatory counseling for repeat death events. Then he reached up and unhooked the monitor leads from his chest.

[1eddw8fo] "Hey," the medic said.

[7cvhxdfh] "I'm leaving," Cage said.

[6lau3sm6] The medic's eyes shifted, not to Cage, but to the air. Waiting for the system to tell him what to do. The system told him nothing fast enough.

[7fuv1465] Cage walked out of the room, down the hallway, past the waiting chairs and the screens and the woman at the counter. The cameras followed him. The ads tried to follow him too.

[pigeyrpw] At the door the shaved-haired girl watched him. "They'll ping you," she said.

[e82nv2yx] "Let them," Cage said.

[vfclca5i] Outside the heat hit him like a wall. The sun was lower now, orange at the edges. In the distance his car still burned, a black thread against the pale land.

[l2bwl3c0] He walked away from the clinic because there was nothing else to do. The world had made death illegal. The only freedom left was to keep walking.
